title: Dungeon Universalis - Take a Look - Part Three
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eymQ0rXs7d8

Part three of Daniel (@dungeondive)'s **Dungeon Universalis** deep dive is the cards — which, given the game's design philosophy, means most of the box.

Pre-made hero cards include nine miniature-equipped narrative protagonists from Oscar's novel (the only good-aligned dwarves can be paladins, ogre and goblin player races exist) plus a stack of additional standee-only heroes spanning bird-folk, frog-folk, gladiator minotaurs, and Don Quixote. Daniel flags the visible inconsistency in art style across cards as a deliberate consequence of using many illustrators — not a flaw, but worth knowing about. The 110+ travel-and-settlement-events deck is what he's most excited about: thematic random encounters keyed to terrain types, plus settlement cards (markets, oracles, blacksmiths, taverns) that turn the campaign map into something Warhammer-Quest-shaped.

The race-and-class deck supports massive combinatorial character creation. The spell deck is split across 16 schools of magic with six spells each, full unique art on every card, and the corruption school's *Entrails Blast* (turn an ally into a living bomb) gets the on-camera reaction it deserves. Mercenary cards and pet cards. The special-elements and furniture decks are the *real* prize for emergent quest design — each card details rules and interactions for shrines, fountains, mirrors, throne rooms, libraries, torture machines.

Plus dark-player decks (powers, encounters, obstacles), AI-control cards for monster classes, treasure, magical objects, mundane weapons, armours, and three skill decks. "This game," Daniel concludes, "is just like a ton of variety in the box."

----

Daniel argues the Special Elements and Furniture decks — interactive props as their own deck — are what unlock player-created quests in this design. Have you played a dungeon crawl that handled environmental interactivity well, or is it always the gap between board game and RPG?
